_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: He arrived broke, borrowed someone else's coin, and decided he'd run the table anyway.

museum_objects.title: 1794 Flowing Hair Silver Dollar
museum_objects.description: One of the first silver dollars struck by the United States Mint under the Coinage Act of 1792.
museum_objects.significance: This coin is Conrad's actual birth certificate — the moment a brand-new country decided it would print its confidence into metal.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the very first US silver dollars ever made, from the year the dollar officially became America's currency.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Continental Dollar
museum_objects.description: An experimental coin issued during the American Revolution, predating the official U.S. dollar by 16 years.
museum_objects.significance: Before Conrad had a country behind him, he was already trying to convince people he was worth something — this coin is his awkward, uncertain first draft.
museum_objects.plain_language: An experimental coin from the Revolutionary War, made before the U.S. dollar officially existed.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: 1793 Chain Cent
museum_objects.description: One of the very first coins struck by the newly established United States Mint, featuring a 15-link chain design.
museum_objects.significance: The chain was supposed to symbolize unity between states — people hated it because it looked like slavery's chains instead. Even Conrad's very first coin sparked controversy.
museum_objects.museum: Smithsonian National Museum of American History
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the very first American pennies ever made — its chain design was so controversial it was redesigned within months.
museum_objects.institution: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

resolved.via: TIER 2 — free API key required (CEO signup)

museum_objects.title: Coinage Act of 1792 (Original Text)
museum_objects.description: The founding legislation that established the U.S. Mint and defined the dollar as the country's monetary unit.
museum_objects.significance: This is the actual law that gave Conrad his name, his job, and his rules — everything about who he is starts in this document.
museum_objects.plain_language: The actual law, still readable today, that created the U.S. dollar and the U.S. Mint.

museum_objects.title: Massachusetts Pine Tree Shilling
museum_objects.description: Colonial-era silver coinage struck in Boston, one of the first coins minted in British North America.
museum_objects.significance: A century before Conrad existed, the colonies were already practicing — proving they could make their own money instead of relying on someone else's.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the first coins ever made in the American colonies, over 100 years before the U.S. dollar existed.

world_map.map_description: Conrad was born at the first U.S. Mint in Philadelphia and grew to become the world's dominant reserve currency, backed by nothing but trust in American institutions.

nodes.label: Philadelphia (First U.S. Mint)
nodes.description: Site of the first official United States Mint, established under the Coinage Act of 1792.

nodes.label: Potosí / Mexico City
nodes.description: Spanish colonial silver dollars circulated widely in early America and directly influenced the design and value of the U.S. dollar.

nodes.label: Boston (Pine Tree Shilling mint)
nodes.description: Site of early colonial coinage that established a precedent for American-made money.

nodes.label: New York (Federal Hall / Wall Street)
nodes.description: Where the U.S. financial system took shape, eventually becoming the center of global dollar-based finance.

nodes.label: Bretton Woods, New Hampshire
nodes.description: Site of the 1944 conference that made the dollar the anchor of the global monetary system.
